entries.change_summary: Reflects two February 2026 FMCSA final rules under 49 CFR Part 393: (1) clarification that FMCSR does not require tire load restriction sidewall markings (doc #2026-03260, eff. 2026-03-23), and (2) a new brake exception for pre-2010 portable conveyors used in aggregate industry operations (doc #2026-03256, eff. 2026-03-23).

The Federal Register document (2026-12448, effective 2026-07-22) rescinds the requirement to keep an ELD operator's manual in the CMV — an amendment to the ELD in-cab documentation rules (which fall under 49 CFR Part 395, Subpart B, specifically §395.22). The audited post makes no claim about the ELD operator's manual requirement, in-cab document retention for ELDs, or §395.22. The post's only specific reference to §395.8 is a general, accurate statement that "certain Hours-of-Service records must be retained under 49 CFR §395.8, which governs record of duty status requirements." That statement remains accurate and is entirely unaffected by the operator's manual rescission. The FR amendment does not alter §395.8's record retention mandates, retention timelines, or any other regulatory fact asserted in the post. No concrete change evidenced in the supplied source data touches any claim actually made in the published post.

entries.change_summary: Two 2026 FMCSA final rules amend 49 CFR Part 396: one explicitly permits electronic DVIRs (effective 2026-03-23), and one revises the requirement to return completed roadside inspection forms to issuing State agencies (effective 2026-07-22). The post has been updated to reflect both changes.

entries.reasoning: Federal Register document 2026-12448 (published 2026-06-22, effective 2026-07-22) is a final rule amending 49 CFR Part 395 to rescind the requirement that a copy of the ELD operator's manual be kept in the CMV. The current post does not mention this requirement at all — neither the original rule nor its rescission. However, because the post is a compliance guide for 49 CFR Part 395 / § 395.22, the rescission of a formerly mandatory in-cab document requirement under that same regulatory framework is a concrete, specific amendment to 49 CFR Part 395 that a compliance-oriented reader relying on this post would need to know about. The post's "Core ELD Compliance Requirements" section and its regulatory reference summary should be updated to note that carriers are no longer required to keep an ELD operator's manual in the CMV (effective 2026-07-22), while drivers still must understand ELD operation to ensure log accuracy and present information during inspections. entries.change_summary: Add a note reflecting the FMCSA final rule (FR doc #2026-03256, eff. 2026-03-23) that creates a limited exception to the all-wheels braking requirement for pre-2010 portable conveyors used in aggregate industry operations, amending 49 CFR Part 393. This prevents the post from implying an unqualified all-wheels brake requirement with no exceptions.

A 2026 final rule (FR doc #2026-12448, eff. 2026-07-22) rescinds the requirement to keep an ELD operator's manual in the CMV under 49 CFR Part 395; the post should reflect this amendment and clarify that the driver knowledge/operation requirement remains in force.

Two 2026 final rules amending 49 CFR Part 396 are reflected: (1) explicit authorization of electronic DVIRs (FR doc #2026-03264, eff. 2026-03-23); and (2) revision of the motor carrier obligation to sign and return completed roadside inspection reports to issuing State agencies (FR doc #2026-12450, eff. 2026-07-22).

entries.reasoning: The Federal Register document in question (doc #2026-03258, effective 2026-03-23) amends 49 CFR Part 391 by removing an obsolete grandfathering provision related to a historical vision waiver study program (1992–1994) and vision exemption program (1998–2022). This is a narrow housekeeping amendment that eliminates a provision that was already effectively inoperative. The published post makes no claims about vision standards, vision waivers, or any grandfathering provisions under 49 CFR Part 391. The only specific regulatory citation in the post touching Part 391 is to §391.11(b)(2) regarding English Language Proficiency, which is entirely unaffected by this amendment. There is no concrete, specific change introduced by doc #2026-03258 that conflicts with, contradicts, or materially updates any factual regulatory claim made in the post.

entries.change_summary: FMCSA's final rule (FR doc #2026-03258, effective 2026-03-23) removes the obsolete grandfathering provision for drivers under the 1992–1994 vision waiver study and 1998–2022 vision exemption programs from 49 CFR Part 391 physical qualification standards. The post's medical qualification section should be updated to reflect that this grandfathering pathway no longer exists under Part 391.

FMCSA final rule (doc #2026-12448, eff. 2026-07-22) rescinds the requirement to keep an ELD operator's manual in the CMV under § 395.22(h); updated Step 5 of the carrier action plan to remove the now-rescinded manual-in-cab instruction and added a note reflecting the regulatory change.

entries.reasoning: Federal Register document #2026-12448 (published 2026-06-22, effective 2026-07-22) is a final FMCSA rule that rescinds the requirement under 49 CFR Part 395 for a copy of the ELD operator's manual to be kept in the CMV. The current post, in Part 1, recommends that carriers ensure drivers have paper log fallback documentation ready for roadside inspection encounters where ELD integrity is questioned, and the ELD product listing is framed around documentation "officers expect" during ELD integrity issues. While the post does not explicitly state that an ELD operator's manual must be kept in the vehicle, the broader regulatory context of 49 CFR Part 395 ELD documentation requirements is materially affected: a concrete amendment to Part 395 is now in effect (as of 2026-07-22) that removes a previously required in-cab document. The post claims regulatory references were "verified as of March 2026" and makes specific claims about what documentation "officers expect" during ELD-related inspections under Part 395 — the rescission of the operator's manual requirement is a concrete, specific amendment to Part 395 that postdates the post's verification date and should be disclosed. This is not speculative; doc #2026-12448 is a final rule with a concrete effective date and a specific regulatory change to Part 395 ELD requirements that a carrier or driver relying on this post's Part 395 compliance guidance would need to know. The only Federal Register document provided (doc #2026-03258, published 2026-02-19, effective 2026-03-23) amends 49 CFR Part 391 to remove an obsolete grandfathering provision related to the vision waiver study program (1992–1994) and vision exemption program (1998–2022) under the physical qualifications standards for interstate drivers. This change is entirely confined to the vision standards subsection of Part 391 and has no bearing whatsoever on § 391.11(b)(2), which governs English language proficiency qualifications. The published post makes no claims about vision standards, grandfathering provisions, or the waiver/exemption programs addressed by that rule. The eCFR fetch was unavailable, so no live text comparison can be made, but per the conservative standard, that absence of data mandates NO_CHANGE rather than speculation. No other Federal Register documents amending § 391.11(b)(2) or 49 CFR Part 395 in a manner relevant to the post's claims have been supplied. Accordingly, there is no concrete, specific change evidenced in the provided source data that the post fails to reflect.

entries.change_summary: Final rule 2026-12448 (eff. 2026-07-22) rescinds the requirement to keep an ELD operator's manual in the CMV in any form; the post incorrectly states that digital availability remains mandatory and must be updated to reflect that no in-vehicle manual is required post-July 22, 2026.

The Federal Register document cited (doc #2026-12448, effective 2026-07-22) amends ELD operator's manual requirements — specifically rescinding the requirement that a physical copy of the ELD operator's manual be kept in the CMV. This change falls under FMCSA's ELD regulations (49 CFR Part 395, Subpart B, specifically §395.22 or related ELD provisions), but the published post does not make any claim about the ELD operator's manual requirement or its storage in the vehicle. The post's only reference to ELDs is a general compliance tip advising drivers to "use your ELD to track remaining drive time" and a link to a separate ELD compliance guide. None of the post's regulatory assertions — the 11-hour driving limit, 14-hour on-duty limit, 30-minute rest break, 60/70-hour weekly limit, or 34-hour restart — are affected by this amendment. Because the post contains no statement that the FR document contradicts, corrects, or supplements, no material update to this post is warranted.
